$comment_1: Hand-written Halvren-voice few-shot examples for the Checklist Live engine.
$comment_2: Four anchors: CCO (uranium, mostly-clean), CNQ (oil/gas, exceptional), AG (silver, mixed-with-fails), ENB (infrastructure, durable).
$comment_3: Each example follows the JSON-Lines protocol the engine produces at runtime: one object per question (q1..q10) plus one final scorecard object.
$comment_4: When updating: keep verdict, text, sources tight; never include marketing words; the voice should sound like the principal at the desk, not a brochure.

lines.text: FCF positive every full year since 2017, including the post-Fukushima trough. The dividend was held when the McArthur shutdown reduced production from 2018 through 2022.

McArthur River and Cigar Lake operate in the low-US$20s per pound on a cash basis. The marginal Canadian and African producers do not. The honest read at trough uranium is that Cameco's mines work and the rest of the cohort does not.
Net cash position at FY 2025. C$1.2B of cash and investments against C$1.0B of total debt. For a cyclical that is a non-trivial place to be.
The 2023 Westinghouse acquisition is the open test. Early EBITDA contribution is encouraging; the durable mix between fuel-services and reactor-build is the part FY 2026 and 2027 will settle.
Insider ownership is modest. Cameco is a public-market story, not an owner-operator one, so the weight falls on capital allocation behaviour rather than direct buys.
2018: mothballed McArthur rather than produce into a depressed price. 2020: held the dividend, no equity raised. The record reads as discipline in both stress windows.
Compensation is per-share-aligned post-2018 reset. Production targets are weighted alongside per-share metrics, not above them.
Tim Gitzel has run Cameco since 2011. Succession is visible at the executive level but not concentrated in one named successor. We keep an eye on it.
On the uranium cost curve that matters — not the pitch-deck version — McArthur and Cigar sit comfortably first-quartile. The denominator is the global mined-uranium cohort.
A mid-cycle realized uranium price is enough to make the business work. The Westinghouse earnings stream is materially less cyclical than the mining stream. The two together earn the wait.

Cameco is the rare uranium name read as an operating business first and a commodity call second. Two amber Pillar II questions (succession, insider activity) sit on top of clean Pillar I and III reads. The desk works further on this name; it would not start from scratch on it.
FCF positive every full year since 2010, including 2015–2016 and 2020. The maintenance free-cash-flow break-even sits below US$40 WTI at current strip differentials.
Long-life, low-decline assets at Horizon and Pelican Lake produce in the C$10s-per-barrel operating range. The mid-cycle WTI does not need to be a peak for the unit economics to work.
Investment-grade. Net debt walked down through every cycle since 2016. The 2020 trough did not require any covenant relief or equity issuance.
Pelican Lake polymer flood, Horizon expansion, and the 2020–2025 buyback velocity all produced per-share-accretive returns. The reinvestment record is among the cleanest in Canadian heavy oil.

Murray Edwards is a meaningful long-term shareholder. The Edwards record of behaviour — discipline at the trough, restraint at the peak — is the cleanest owner-operator signal in the Canadian energy patch.
2015: dividend raised, no equity issued, capex cut hard and selectively. 2020: dividend raised in March, the same week WTI front-month went briefly negative. The record is the receipt.
Compensation is per-share-aligned. Reserves-per-share and free-cash-per-share are tracked metrics. The proxy filings have been consistent on this for a decade.

Tim McKay was promoted to CEO in 2020 with the operational pedigree to continue the Edwards-era discipline. The deeper question is the cultural transmission past the next decade. We watch the board composition.
Sustaining cost places CNQ first-quartile within the Canadian heavy-oil cohort and competitive on the global heavy curve. The denominator is the global heavy-oil mined and produced base.

At mid-cycle WTI (US$65–75) and current Canadian differentials, CNQ compounds meaningfully. The thesis does not require a commodity peak. The decade-out question is the regulatory framework, not the business.

Twenty-six consecutive years of dividend raises through 2015 and 2020. The Edwards capital culture is on the public record. Two ambers (insider concentration is moderate; succession past Edwards is the open question); eight greens elsewhere. The desk reads this as the reference Canadian energy compounder.
FCF has been positive in some years and negative in others over the past decade. The cycle is real and the asset base is not yet stable enough through a full one for the answer to read cleanly.

At silver in the low-US$20s the cohort all-in cost sits at or above the price for most senior silver producers. First Majestic's AISC has been in that range. The unit economics at the trough are uncomfortable.
Balance sheet has been walked back from prior distress but is not yet investment-grade. The 2020 cycle required restraint; a deeper cycle would test the position again.
Reinvestment record on incremental capital has been weak. The Gatos and other transactions extended absolute production at the cost of meaningful per-share dilution. The math is in the proxy.
Keith Neumeyer is the founder and remains a meaningful holder. Open-market activity has been mixed. The signal is partial.
2015 and 2020 both required equity raises at meaningfully below prior peaks. The dividend has been intermittent. The record is mixed; it is not clean.
Compensation has historically been weighted toward absolute production and reserve growth rather than per-share metrics. The proxy structure is part of why the per-share record has lagged.
Succession bench is not publicly named in a way that supports confident underwriting beyond the current founder-CEO. The company is built around one principal.

On a global silver-equivalent cost curve First Majestic sits in the middle-to-back half. The senior pure-play silver cohort is broadly there; the by-product silver from polymetallic mines is structurally cheaper.
At mid-cycle silver of US$22–25 the business breaks even on a free-cash basis. The thesis requires the upper half of the silver price band to compound. We do not buy thesis-requires-the-peak names.

Three reds (unit economics at trough, reinvestment record, compensation structure) on top of six ambers and one moderate-positive read on the founder presence. The honest read is not yet a position — and the desk has held it as a forcing function for the per-share framework, not as a long. The streamer model is the structural workaround for silver.
FCF positive every full year since the 2017 Spectra combination. The contracted EBITDA structure decouples earnings from commodity price.
Approximately 98% of EBITDA is take-or-pay, cost-of-service, or fee-based. The trough commodity quarter has not historically moved the operating earnings meaningfully.
Net debt is meaningfully above the 4.5× target band after the 2023–2024 Dominion utility acquisitions. The walk back is in progress; the target is publicly committed.
The Mainline expansion years produced acceptable ROIC; the Dominion acquisitions are the open question. The 2026–2027 utility rate-case outcomes will settle it.
Insider ownership is modest. Enbridge is an institutional name; the structural signal is the capital allocation behaviour at the board rather than direct insider buys.
2015: dividend raised. 2020: dividend raised. No equity issued at distressed prices in either window. The record is in the public filings.
Compensation has been per-share-tilted since the 2018 reset. The DCF-per-share metric is a tracked compensation input.
Greg Ebel succeeded Al Monaco cleanly in 2023. The executive bench is named and visible. Succession is not the open question on this name.
The Mainline corridor between the WCSB and the US Midwest does not get rebuilt every cycle. Rate-base economics dominate the cost-curve discussion.
At any realistic North American natural gas demand path and any reasonable Canadian heavy oil path, the contracted base earns. The decade-out question is regulatory, not commodity.

Three Pillar II ambers (debt post-Dominion, ROIC on the acquisitions, modest insider) sit alongside seven greens. Thirty-one years of dividend raises is the receipt. The desk reads ENB as a reference toll-road compounder; the regulatory environment is the open question we track quarterly.
